behaviors.description: TSP heuristic produces routes ≤1.5x optimal length for inputs up to 20 stops
behaviors.justification: This is a pure algorithmic correctness test. The failure mode is 'heuristic produces suboptimal routes'. We can create synthetic coordinate sets (hardcoded or generated) and verify the algorithm's output against known optimal solutions or benchmarks for small instances. No I/O, no external dependencies, no mocking of the algorithm itself—we run the actual code under test. A Unit test directly measures algorithm quality.
behaviors.test_description: Create 5–10 test cases with 5–20 stops at known coordinates. For each, compute the heuristic route length and verify it is ≤1.5× the optimal length (which can be precomputed or looked up from TSP benchmark datasets). Assert that all test cases pass the ratio threshold.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit level is consistent. No external API, no network, no I/O—just algorithm correctness.

behaviors.description: Delivery time windows computed correctly from distance and configured average speed
behaviors.justification: Pure mathematical calculation: time = distance ÷ speed (adjusted by road type). No I/O, no external dependencies. Can mock distance matrix, mock road-type speed config, and verify the formula is applied correctly. A mock cannot hide this failure—if the formula is wrong (wrong operator, wrong operands, unit mismatch), the test fails. Unit level is sufficient.
behaviors.test_description: Mock a distance matrix (e.g., [[0, 10, 20], [10, 0, 15], [20, 15, 0]]) and speed config (e.g., highway=60km/h, local=30km/h). For each pair of stops, verify calculated time = distance ÷ speed. Test edge cases: zero distance, very high speed, mixed road types.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit level is consistent. Pure calculation with no external dependencies.

behaviors.description: Shift-hours validator rejects routes exceeding remaining shift hours and returns excess amount
behaviors.justification: Pure validation logic: compare total route time against remaining shift hours. No I/O, no external calls. Can mock route times and shift hours. A Unit test directly verifies the validation condition and return value. A mock cannot hide this—if the comparison is reversed or the excess calculation is wrong, the test fails.
behaviors.test_description: Create test routes with different total times (e.g., 6h, 8h, 10h) and shift hours (e.g., 8h remaining). For each, verify: (1) the validator rejects routes exceeding the shift (e.g., 10h route rejected for 8h shift), (2) accepted routes are ≤shift hours, (3) excess amount returned equals total_time − remaining_shift_hours. Test boundary: a route exactly matching remaining hours should be accepted.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit level is consistent. Pure comparison logic, no I/O.

behaviors.description: When geocoding fails for an address (API returns no results), service skips it, optimizes remaining addresses, and reports which were skipped
behaviors.justification: This behavior depends on real API integration. The failure modes are (1) service doesn't skip failed addresses, (2) service doesn't optimize remaining ones, and (3) service doesn't report skipped addresses—all addressable by Unit test with mocked API. BUT: a mock-based test risks missing integration failures. The real geocoding API may return a different error format (e.g., HTTP 404, empty array, custom error object) than our mock. If the code checks `if result is None` but the real API returns `{}`, the mock test passes but production fails. This is a MOCK-MISS. To catch both business-logic bugs AND API-integration bugs, we must test with the real API or a recorded/realistic response. Minimum level: Integration.
behaviors.test_description: Call the real geocoding API with a list containing valid addresses and one that does not exist (or use a recorded/stub response). Verify: (1) the non-existent address is skipped (no stop created), (2) the remaining addresses are optimized into a route, (3) the service returns the list of skipped address identifiers. Confirm the service does not crash and correctly handles the API's actual failure response.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Integration level is justified. Cannot rely on mock if the real API's error format is unknown or differs from assumed.

behaviors.description: Duplicate addresses in input are consolidated into single stops with combined delivery items, not visited twice
behaviors.justification: Deduplication and item consolidation is business logic, not I/O or external integration. Can test with synthetic address lists. The failure mode is 'duplicates are not detected or items are not combined'. A Unit test can directly verify: (1) duplicate detection (string match, geocoding match, or configured tolerance), (2) item consolidation (count, aggregation), and (3) resulting route contains only one stop per unique address. No mock would hide this—if the logic is wrong, the test fails.
behaviors.test_description: Create an input list with duplicate addresses (exact matches and possibly case-insensitive variants if applicable) and multiple items assigned to each address. Verify: (1) the service consolidates all duplicates into single stops, (2) items from all duplicate entries are combined (e.g., total item count equals sum of individual counts), (3) the optimized route visits each unique location exactly once, and (4) no address is visited multiple times.
behaviors.plan_consistent_note: Unit level is consistent. Pure deduplication and consolidation logic, no external dependencies or I/O.
